responseSet.title: Reading Winnicott edited by Lesley Caldwell, Angela Joyce Routledge, Hove and New York, 2011, New Library of Psychoanalysis Teaching Series; 336 pp; £65.00/$99.00
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Reading Winnicott edited by Lesley Caldwell, Angela Joyce Routledge, Hove and New York, 2011, New Library of Psychoanalysis Teaching Series; 336 pp; £65.00/$99.00

Steven Groarke

Steven Groarke

80 Roehampton Lane,
London SW15 5SL
E-mail: s.groarke@roehampton.ac.uk

Steven Groarke  

There are no unexpected choices in this judicious selection of seminal papers, except perhaps for the inclusion of A clinical study of the effect of a failure of the average expectable environment on a child's mental functioning (1965). The paper, which on the editors' own reckoning is little read, gives an account of a therapeutic consultation with a 6 year-old boy. We are prompted to ask why a paper that is not much read finds its way into an introductory reader where there is an obvious premium on space. The question of inclusion, however, turns out to be more interesting than a quibble over a hierarchy of preferences. It is not simply a matter of drawing attention to a less prominent paper, either. While not on a par with some of the other papers in the collection, nonetheless, Winnicott's consultation with the child foregrounds the different horizons of meaning in his clinical thinking. As with The Piggle (1977), and not only because they both comprise case material, it is a paper in which what is said is what is happening. We find Winnicott here doing all he knew how to do. More than anything else, this means allowing for the child to reach something of his own making, a reach for meaning out of himself. Getting to the heart of what these papers are about, the therapeutic consultation demonstrates that Winnicott was not interested in interpreting the drawings so much as understanding by drawing.

We are brought back in this case and by the commentaries on each of the 14 papers included in the book, to the historical conjuncture in which the papers were initially presented. Of course, the introductory notes to each of the papers do not amount to anything like the “scholarly history of Winnicott and the reception of his ideas in British psychoanalysis” that the editors call for in the conclusion to their general introduction. But the book certainly meets the requirements of the New Library of Psychoanalysis Teaching Series to provide a comprehensive yet accessible introduction to Winnicott without oversimplifying matters.

responseSet.title: Clinical Work with Emptiness and Sexuality: A Commentary on Maurizio Balsamo's Paper ‘Sabina’
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Clinical Work with Emptiness and Sexuality: A Commentary on Maurizio Balsamo's Paper ‘Sabina’

Lina Balestriere

Lina Balestriere

Brussels, Belgium
E-mail: lina.balestriere@apsyucl.be

Lina Balestriere  

I was very pleased to read Maurizio Balsamo's highly subtle description of Sabina's analysis. Without further ado, he invites us into the analyst's consulting-room in order to follow, step by step, the work that he does during a session. We then immediately find ourselves in contact with the heavy atmosphere that reigns in the room - the heaviness of the transference and of the countertransference to which it gives rise. Then comes the time for interpretation - surprised by it, we cannot but come to life again and get down to work. We are struck by the differentiation that the analyst makes and by the tenacity of the further processing that he undertakes in the face of the defensive movement that counters his interpretation. We are moved by the feeling of pleasure that manages to emerge and take its place beside the deadliness that tries to put a stop to everything that may transpire. Maurizio Balsamo allows us to accompany the work of transforming his countertransference feelings that he was able to accomplish during the session and to travel along the remarkable interpretative pathways that he opens up. Those countertransference feelings were pushing the analyst aside, anger was never far from incapacity, and sleep was coming close to absence. Rooted firmly in distancing and in falling asleep, and processing both of these, Maurizio Balsamo's interpretation was indeed very lively. Lively and also mobilizing, because it succeeded in functioning like a ‘libidinal cathexis’ at the precise moment when the analyst was about to abandon the search for anything that could be libidinally cathected, just when decathexis was waiting to take over. On the edge of absence and decathexis, there came an interpretation that in several ways was inventive: the passive and passivizing emptiness became the emptiness of someone, of an agent; the massively engulfing emptiness was split up into “two ways”, “two things”; the emptiness of death was distinguished from what stood right next to it - not death, but pleasure. By limiting the emptiness and its melancholic power, the analyst's words made “good things” come alive and enabled a transference cathexis: “I love you”.

responseSet.title: Reconsidering Therapeutic Action: Loewald, Cognitive Neuroscience and the Integration of Memory's Duality
responseSet.authorMast: Jefferson A. Singer and  Martin A. Conway
responseSet.authorCitation: Singer, J. A. & Conway, M. A.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Reconsidering Therapeutic Action: Loewald, Cognitive Neuroscience and the Integration of Memory's Duality

Jefferson Singer and Martin Conway

Martin Conway

New London, CT 06320, USA - jasin@conncoll.edu

Leeds, LS2 9JT, UK - M.A.Conway@leeds.ac.uk

Martin Conway  

memory, Loewald, self-defining, memories, cognitive, neuroscience, self-memory, system

(Final version accepted 22 November 2010)

Both Loewald's relational theory of memory and the Self-Memory System (SMS) of cognitive neuroscience describe a dual memory system, one system that is experience-near sensory-perceptual, and the other, symbolic and conceptual. In contrast to perspectives that locate therapeutic action in either altering implicit procedural memories or interpreting explicit historical content, we argue that psychological health emerges from effective integration of both memory systems, achieved through a combination of transference dynamics and analytic insight. We support this position by elaborating four key assumptions of the Loewaldian and SMS perspectives, followed by application to a clinical example. We highlight the power of certain integrative autobiographical memories called ‘self-defining memories’ in assisting an understanding of transference dynamics and providing metaphoric touchstones to guide subsequent treatment.

responseSet.title: Rudi Vermote's Response to David Taylor
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Rudi Vermote's Response to David Taylor

Rudi Vermote

Rudi Vermote

Leuvense steenweg 517, Kortenberg 3070, Belgium - rudi.vermote@uc-kortenberg.be

Rudi Vermote  

I thank David Taylor for his delicate and thoughtful discussion. In it he explains his preference for the so called ‘mid-Bion’ who studied the thinking or psychic processing of experiences, combining sharp clinical observation with the application of scientific models. One of the geometrical models that Bion relied on, is Poincaré's theory. The way in which Taylor illustrates how Bion used this theory for his own theory of transformations makes his commentary an outstanding contribution to the study of Bion's work.

Taylor further discusses how the ‘late-Bion’ changed his focus to O and he rephrases this shift in order to keep it clear of the jargon which it has generated over the years. He talks of knowing versus being and of O as the ontological power. Taylor's change of metaphors is enlivening but it runs the risk that we get a false sense of being on more familiar grounds and it may load Bion's theory with philosophical connotations to being that Bion did not himself refer to. As I understand Bion and as I experience it, O is a zone that we can consciously and unconsciously be in contact with.

Taylor's main criticism of the ‘late-Bion’ is that he saw being, as unknowable and that he became less sharp in his clinical observations and had less scientific rigor when apprehending being. In this Taylor seems to represent the opinion of many colleagues (O'Shaugnessy, 2005).

I enjoyed Taylor's comment but from his response, I get the impression that the most important point that I tried to convey about the late-Bion did not come through in my text. Taylor's commentary gives me the opportunity to formulate my point about Transformations in O and psychic change more strongly. For reasons of space, I do not mention here the many coexisting and important elaborations of vertices of Bion's work, like those of Ferro, Grotstein, Ogden, Britton to name only a few. My point is that while ‘mid-Bion’ is concerned with the psychic processing (or thinking) of emotional experiences, ‘late-Bion’ presents pivotal ideas on the heart of psychic change and consequently develops a discomforting and radical technique. While ‘mid-Bion’ is about transforming psychic experiences into representations and the further transformations of these psychic representations, ‘late-Bion’ aims to operate at the conjunctions (or patterns) which are not yet expressed in sensuous, emotional, relational phenomena (or, as Taylor states, ‘dim apprehensions of something which seems central to the patient’). Bion in fact discerned three levels of psychic functioning which are not so different from current neuropsychological findings on cortical, prefrontal, basal limbic functions. First there is a level of logical thinking, understanding (Reason), second a level of creative spontaneous associative dream verbal thought (Idea or Knowledge)

responseSet.title: Between the Imaginary and the Real: Photographic Portraits of Mourning and of Melancholia in Argentina
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Between the Imaginary and the Real: Photographic Portraits of Mourning and of Melancholia in Argentina

Julia Reineman

Julia Reineman

11025 Estacia Lane, Yucaipa, CA 92399, USA - Dynamic.Spznish@hotmail.com

Julia Reineman  

photography, mourning, linking objects, Lacan, Brodsky, Ulanovsky, Madres de Plaza de Mayo, Argentina, ‘Dirty War’, desaparecidos

(Final version accepted 4 November 2010)

During Argentina's ‘Dirty War’ (1976-83), the military regime attempted to erase an entire population; today the photographs of the dead/missing stand in defiance, contradicting that attempted erasure of the desaparecidos. In this essay, I explain the connection between photography and loss, and how photography fits within Lacan's understanding of the Imaginary, the Symbolic, the Real, and the ‘gaze’. I discuss complicated mourning (circumstances which inhibit/delay mourning) and the difficulties created by political disappearances: as long as the family members maintain the belief that their loved one(s) might still be alive, they cannot begin the process of mourning the permanently lost object. Beginning with the Madres de Plaza de Mayo and, using the web-based art exhibits of Marcelo Brodsky and Inés Ulanovsky, I analyze the role of the photograph in Argentina, how it serves as a linking object, how it is used to symbolize the dead/missing, and how it can function to facilitate mourning, or to serve as proof of pathological melancholia. I argue that such artistic representations of loss function to reinscribe healthy mourning rituals within the Argentine society.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Catharsis: Psychoanalysis and the Theatre

Jean-Michel Vives

Jean-Michel Vives

90 Chemin Beau Site 83100 Toulon, France - vives@unice.fr

Jean-Michel Vives  

catharsis, desire, psychoanalytic cure, tragedy, unveiling

(Final version accepted 27 September 2010)

The notion of catharsis, in relation to tragedy, was introduced by Aristotle in his work Poetics. Over the centuries, Aristotle's innovative and enigmatic reference to this process has been widely commented on and given rise to intense controversy. In 1895, Freud and Breuer reconsidered this notion in their Studies on Hysteria, where they present the so-called cathartic therapeutic method. It is not, however, this aspect of psychoanalytical theory that the author of this article seeks to elucidate: drawing on a detailed study of the references to tragic catharsis in the work of Freud and Lucan, the author proposes to examine their implications for psychoanalytic treatment. With specific reference to Freud's article Psychopathic characters on the stage (1905) and Lacan's commentary on Sophocles’ Antigone (1960), the author argues that catharsis is to be understood not so much as a mechanism of discharge linked to abreaction, but rather as the actual analytic process itself during which the Subject is ‘unveiled’ and thus faced with the enigma of his own desire.

[[RunningHead]]

Finding the Depressed Object in the Obsessional: A Clinical Study

Claire Cripwell

Claire Cripwell

5 Beacon Hill London N7 9LY — clairecripwell@hotmail.com

Claire Cripwell  

Keywords: anxiety, depression, enactment, obsession, transference

In this paper the author explores the clinical significance of the presence of a depressed internal object in a patient with marked obsessional features, dominating the patient's internal world and restricting relations in external life. After discussing important aspects of the contribution of Freud and later writers to the study of obsessional neurosis, the author provides clinical material that shows the patient's tormented relationship to a feared depressed object that was manifested in the transference. Developing her argument, the author suggests that if the analyst does not fully grasp the primitive anxieties of the underlying state of mind she can be prone to enter into an aggressive enactment with the patient's sadistic superego. This kind of enactment may arouse excitement and triumph in the patient, but actually confirms his doubts and fears about the capacity of his object to contain him.

[[RunningHead]]

Reading French Psychoanalysis edited by Dana Birksted-Breen, Sara Flanders, Alain Gibeault Routledge, London and New York 2010, New Library of Psychoanalysis; 817 pp; £39.95

Alfred Margulies

Alfred Margulies

1493 Cambridge Street, Cambridge, MA 02139, USA
E-mail: alfred_margulies@hms.harvard.edu

Alfred Margulies  

In search of French Psychoanalysis

Not everything is expressible in every country.

(Stanley Cavell, 1988, p. 67)

The enigmatic message, or the dream of Lacan's injection

At the mysterious heart of Freud's ‘dream of Irma's injection’, a dream that launches The Interpretation of Dreams and so psychoanalysis itself, lies a stuttering, enigmatic symbol: “propyl, propyls … proprionic acid … tri-methlamin (and I saw before me the formula for this printed in heavy type)” (Freud, 1900, p. 107). Trimethylamine, Freud had learned from Fliess, is “one of the products of sexual metabolism” (Freud, 1900, p. 116); according to Lacan: “Indeed - I've made inquiries - trimethylamine is a decomposition product of sperm, and it gives it its ammoniacal smell when it's left to decompose in the air” (Lacan, 1991, p. 158).’ And so, at the heart of this seminal dream of psychoanalysis, a dream space that penetrates into the mysteries of sexuality and a woman's body, lies a literally seminal symbol which, given its importance to Freud (and French psychoanalysis), penetrates into the origins itself of psychoanalysis. The structural formula of this molecular symbol is, uncannily, a multiply branched chemical ‘tree’.

At least uncannily to me because, to my eye, a similar, strangely disem-bodied, entangled and mysterious tree of French psychoanalytic genealogy (a tree of who analyzed whom) introduces this extraordinary book, a book that quite literally begins with the complex enigma of its own origins and paternity and the uniquely traumatic question of French psychoanalytic history itself. Who is the father?

Of course, we know who the grandfather is, Freud himself. Nevertheless, the origins of the distinctive, urbane style of French psychoanalysis - philo-sophically sophisticated with rich, complex, non-linear conceptions of language, time, desire and memory - remain controversial, disputed, and still unassimilated. Like the uncanny branching symbol of the Irma dream, this chart of many fathers - with its intricate and knotted paternity - begins the book and pervades it.
